text: The surgeon operates with the camera positioned in the left upper quadrant and the right hand in the right upper quadrant, using two left-sided trocars. The omentum is being removed from the area of disease, revealing the terminal ileum and cecum adhered to the right dome of the bladder and the right pelvic sidewall. The surgeon inspects for interloop fistulas and observes adherence to the sigmoid colon, aiming to preserve the sigmoid colon during dissection.

The surgeon carefully separates the sigmoid colon, which is plastered to the ileocolic valve, revealing a fistula. The loop extends into the pelvis and returns, with the distal sigmoid above the rectum adhered to the lateral aspect of the ileum. As separation progresses, the surgeon enters a granulation-filled cavity, leading to the decision to resect the sigmoid colon due to unavoidable injury.
The surgeon examines the ileocolic valve and the surrounding diseased tissue, preparing to perform a resection and anastomosis of the sigmoid colon.

The surgeon performs blunt dissection and focused diathermy to expose the extent of the disease, preparing for sigmoid resection. A sponge is placed to manage oozing from inflammatory parts of the fistula. The surgeon completes lateral mobilization and selects the proximal sigmoid margin near the fistula, marking it for reference before taking the mesentery.

The surgeon completes the proximal and distal division of the sigmoid colon and sets the resected specimen aside. The sigmoid anastomosis is performed using an inner layer of VLOC sutures and an outer layer of PDS sutures.
The surgeon completes the anastomosis despite challenging angles, ensuring the connection is secure. A leak test is performed to verify the integrity of the anastomosis.

The surgeon observes a healthy, non-bleeding, widely patent anastomosis. The robotic system is redocked in a standard configuration for a right colectomy or ileocolic resection. Five ports are utilized, including two twelve millimeter trocars for stapling, with one suprapubic port used for extraction. The surgeon runs the bowel proximally, noting the transition to more normal bowel and confirming the extent of the ileocolic resection.
The surgeon continues the lateral to medial dissection plane, avoiding high ligation of the mesentery. The colon margin is selected for resection.

The surgeon marks the small bowel margin and creates a window at the proximal site, then proceeds to take the mesentery, wrapping around to the distal margin. The dissection stays close to the bowel wall, navigating around the branches of the ileocolic pedicle and wrapping towards the colon mesentery.

The surgeon uses a robotic approach to divide the colon and small bowel mesentery, preparing for anastomosis in a minimally invasive procedure for Crohn's disease with fistulizing complications. The surgical field shows the divided colon and mesentery, with the robotic instruments facilitating precise movements.

The surgeon completes the intracorporeal anastomosis by suturing the inner layer of the common channel closure, checking ICG for confirmation, and imbricating the second layer over the first. The suture holding the valve is adjusted for better placement.

The surgeon covers the distal anastomosis with healthy omentum and extracts both specimens through the Pfannenstiel incision, which corresponds to one of the twelve millimeter trocar sites. The procedure concludes without diverting the patient or leaving a drain, and the Foley and stents are removed upon extubation.
